---
title: "Validation"
subtitle: "Closed-form vs. Monte Carlo cross-check, plus the tail metrics the closed form cannot give"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: right
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .metric-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(180px, 1fr)); gap: 0.75rem; margin: 1rem 0; }
  .metric-grid .card { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; padding: 0.75rem; background: #fafafa; }
  .metric-grid .card .label { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #555; }
  .metric-grid .card .value { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 1.2rem; }
  .callout-note-lite { border-left: 3px solid #4c78a8; background: #f3f7fb; padding: 0.6rem 0.9rem; margin: 0.8rem 0; font-size: 0.95rem; }
  .legend-table { font-size: 0.9rem; margin: 0.5rem 0 1rem; }
  .legend-table td:first-child { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; padding-right: 0.8rem; }
</style>
```

The closed-form identities on the [Model](model.qmd) page are the
anchors of this page, and the simulator is the instrument. Each
section below takes one identity — a mean, a variance ratio, a
scaling law, the Merton compensation — and checks the Monte Carlo
against it. Tail metrics that have no closed form (VaR, CVaR, loss
probability) are reported alongside so the comparison lands in the
same view. All computation runs in TypeScript under `src/` and emits
the JSON this page reads; regenerate with `npm run simulate -- --seed
42 && npm run sweep -- --seed 42`.

The scorecard convention: the $z$ column is the primary sanity check
($|z| \le 2$ healthy, $|z| > 3$ points at a simulator bug), and the
variance ratio $\mathrm{Var}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}] /
\mathrm{Var}[R_{\mathrm{fee}}] = (P/f)^2$ between b2b and fee is
algebraic — any disagreement there is a bug, not a sampling artifact.
The matched-desk identity $b2b + \mathrm{treasury}(N P, N P S_0) =
N (Q - P S_0)$ is pathwise deterministic, and the syndicated
operating book satisfies $\mathrm{SD}[\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}] = (1 -
\beta) \, \mathrm{SD}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$ exactly; both are regression
oracles in this page. P&L is the horizon total in USD; divide by $N =
\lambda T$ for a per-tonne figure. All risk-metric definitions live
on [Model →
Risk metrics](model.qmd#risk-metrics).

## One parameter setting, full distributions

This block fixes all parameters and shows the full P&L distribution
of every book. The [sweep below](#parameter-sweep) is the
complementary view — one desk, one risk statistic, parameters sweep.
Data is loaded from `data/run-42.json`.


```{ojs}
//| echo: false
run = FileAttachment("data/run-42.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
import { formatTickCurrency } from "./lib/compiled/src/core/index.js"
```

### Run parameters

$(\mu, \sigma, T)$ set the price process, $\lambda$ the throughput,
$(f, Q, \alpha)$ the business levers. The break-even quote $Q^*$ is
derived from the others and is the point at which fee and b2b share
expected revenue (their distributions still differ — see
[direct comparison](model.qmd#direct-comparison)).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Monte Carlo paths</div><div class="value">${run.params.nPaths.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Time steps / path</div><div class="value">${run.params.nSteps}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">μ (drift, /yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.mu}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">σ (vol, /√yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.sigma}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">T (horizon, yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.T}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">λ (tonnes/yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.lambda.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">f (fee rate)</div><div class="value">${run.params.f}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q (USD/tonne)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.params.Q}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">α (coverage)</div><div class="value">${run.params.alpha}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q* (break-even)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.closed.QStar.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### Scorecard: closed form vs Monte Carlo

One row per operating book — fee, b2b, retained (syndicated-on-b2b),
switching when a threshold is active — plus the treasury. Left
block: closed-form moments versus their MC estimates, with the $z$
column checking their agreement. Right block: tail metrics that have
no closed form. The switching row prints `NaN` in the scorecard's
closed-form moment columns (no closed-form P&L density), but its
mean-level anchors $\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$, $\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$,
$\mathbb{E}[T_{\mathrm{fee}}]$, and $\mathbb{E}[I_{\mathrm{fee}}]$
are tracked in dedicated panels below.

<div class="legend-table">

| column | meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (cf) / (mc) | closed-form and Monte Carlo mean |
| $\pm \mathrm{CI}_{95}$ | 95% half-width of the MC mean, $1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SD} / \sqrt{n}$ |
| SD (cf) / (mc) | closed-form and MC standard deviation |
| $z$ | $(\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{mc}}[\Pi] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{cf}}[\Pi]) / \mathrm{stderr}$ — primary sanity check |
| $\mathrm{VaR}_{95} / \mathrm{VaR}_{99}$ | loss thresholds at the 5% / 1% quantile, reported as positive USD (on the fee book: "low end of revenue" — $R_{\mathrm{fee}} \ge 0$ by construction) |
| $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95} / \mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$ | tail-mean losses inside those thresholds |
| $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ | probability of a losing realisation |
| Sharpe | $\mathbb{E}[\Pi] / \mathrm{SD}[\Pi]$ — horizon-absolute (not annualised); $f$-invariant on the fee book |

</div>

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.rows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "book",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

### Desk compositions — operating + treasury

`matched` is the deterministic desk total $N(Q - P S_0)$ (the
treasury's consumption integral cancels b2b's $- P \lambda I_T$
exactly at $\alpha = 1$). `partial_desk` composes
$b2b + \text{treasury}$ at `run.params.alpha`; its closed form uses
the strong-Markov decomposition of $J_\alpha$. `syndicated_matched`
composes `retained + treasury(N·P, N·P·S₀)` — still deterministic
up to the syndication premium. `switching_matched` composes
`switching + treasury(N·P, N·P·S₀)` and has no closed form (τ is a
stopping time).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
deskRows = [
  {
    name: "matched",
    closedFormMean: run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    closedFormSd: run.desks.matched.closedFormSd,
    mcMean: run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    mcSd: 0,
    mcCi95: 0,
    zScore: 0,
    var95: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    var99: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    cvar95: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    cvar99: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    probLoss: run.desks.matched.closedFormMean < 0 ? 1 : 0,
    sharpe: null,
  },
  run.desks.partial,
  run.desks.syndicatedMatched,
  ...(run.desks.switchingMatched ? [run.desks.switchingMatched] : []),
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(deskRows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "desk",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => Number.isFinite(x) ? (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2) : "—",
    mcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => Number.isFinite(x) ? "$" + x.toFixed(2) : "—",
    mcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

### Merton jumps: means invariant, variances inflated

The [compensated Merton overlay](model.qmd#compensated-merton-jump-diffusion)
has two signatures. Means should be identical to pure GBM — the
compensation $-\lambda_J \kappa$ with $\kappa = e^{\mu_J + \sigma_J^2
/ 2} - 1$ exists precisely to preserve $\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu
t}$. Variances should not — jumps genuinely fatten the tail. The
table below checks both at a canonical overlay ($\lambda_J = 3$/yr,
$\mu_J = -0.1$, $\sigma_J = 0.15$) by comparing the overlay's MC
moments against the GBM closed form. Expect $|z| \lesssim 2$ against
the GBM closed form (compensation firing) and SD (merton) strictly
above SD (gbm); a flat SD column means the overlay is inert.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.jumpCheck.rows, {
  columns: ["name","gbmClosedMean","mertonMcMean","mertonMcCi95","gbmClosedSd","mertonMcSd","zVsGbmClosed"],
  header: {
    name: "book",
    gbmClosedMean: "E[Π] gbm-cf",
    mertonMcMean: "E[Π] merton-mc",
    mertonMcCi95: "±CI95",
    gbmClosedSd: "SD gbm-cf",
    mertonMcSd: "SD merton-mc",
    zVsGbmClosed: "z vs gbm-cf"
  },
  format: {
    gbmClosedMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    gbmClosedSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mertonMcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zVsGbmClosed: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
  }
})
```

_Overlay set by the CLI's `JUMP_CHECK` constant. Matching means
is the compensation identity $\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu t}$
firing; the widened SD column is the jumps landing._

Spot-check on the same run, at the terminal spot:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] gbm closed</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.gbmClosed.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] merton mc</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.mertonMcMean.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">z vs gbm closed</div><div class="value">${(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed).toFixed(2)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### P&L distributions, one panel per stochastic book

Terminal P&L on the x-axis; solid black rule is the closed-form mean
and the dashed grey rule is $\Pi = 0$. Mass to the left of the grey
rule is the loss probability $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ reported in the
scorecard. The fee panel is non-negative and right-skewed (it
inherits $I_T$'s right skew). The b2b panel is the mirror image —
same kernel with opposite sign, upside capped at $Q N$, downside
unbounded. The treasury panel sits around its opening MTM; the
partial-desk panel is `b2b + treasury` composed sample-wise.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Matched desk is deterministic (spike at N·(Q − P·S_0)), so we omit it
// from the per-book panel. The switching row appears only when the fixture
// was generated with a finite barrier.
pnlBooks = [
  "fee", "b2b", "retained", "treasury", "partial_desk",
  ...(run.sampleTraces.switching ? ["switching"] : []),
]

pnlData = [
  ...run.sampleTraces.fee.map(v => ({book: "fee", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.b2b.map(v => ({book: "b2b", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.retained.map(v => ({book: "retained", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.treasury.map(v => ({book: "treasury", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.partialDesk.map(v => ({book: "partial_desk", pnl: v})),
  ...(run.sampleTraces.switching
    ? run.sampleTraces.switching.map(v => ({book: "switching", pnl: v}))
    : []),
]

// Mean rules: operating-book rows ship closed-form means in run.rows; the
// partial-desk row ships its closed-form mean in run.desks.partial.
meanRuleRows = [
  ...run.rows
    .filter(r => pnlBooks.includes(r.name))
    .map(r => ({ book: r.name, x: Number.isFinite(r.closedFormMean) ? r.closedFormMean : r.mcMean })),
  { book: "partial_desk", x: run.desks.partial.closedFormMean },
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 520,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginRight: 30,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "terminal P&L Π  (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  fy: { label: "book" },
  color: { legend: true, domain: pnlBooks, label: "book" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.rectY(pnlData, Plot.binX({ y: "proportion" }, { x: "pnl", fill: "book", fy: "book", thresholds: 60 })),
    Plot.ruleX(meanRuleRows, { x: "x", fy: "book", stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: closed-form $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$. Dashed grey: $\Pi = 0$._

### $I_T$ itself — the kernel behind everything

Fee and b2b are linear in $I_T = \int_0^T S_t \, dt$, and the
partial-desk variance depends on the strong-Markov decomposition of
the uncovered tail. Dufresne (2001) gives mean and variance in
closed form, plotted as rules on top of the MC histogram below. A
converged MC puts about 68% of the mass between the dashed rules;
tail quantiles have no closed form (since $I_T$ is not log-normal)
and are left to Monte Carlo.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
itHist = {
  const { edges, counts } = run.itSampleHistogram;
  const total = counts.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0);
  return counts.map((c,i) => ({
    x0: edges[i], x1: edges[i+1], density: c / (total * (edges[i+1]-edges[i]))
  }));
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "I_T = ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt   ((USD/kVCM)·year)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(itHist, { x1: "x0", x2: "x1", y: "density", fill: "#4c78a8", fillOpacity: 0.7 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean - Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance),
                run.closed.IT.mean + Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance)],
               { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3" })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: closed-form $\mathbb{E}[I_T]$. Dashed: $\pm \mathrm{SD}[I_T]$._

### The matched treasury's interim NAV

The matched desk ($b2b + \text{treasury}(N P, N P S_0)$) has
deterministic terminal P&L, but the treasury's mark-to-market is not
deterministic interim. $V_t = N P (1 - t/T) \cdot S_t$ — remaining
inventory times spot — is a scaled GBM that pins to zero at $T$ (the
burn schedule drains the inventory) but can carve out a material
interim dip on a mid-horizon crash. The sampled trajectories below
show the qualitative shape before the shortfall plot formalises it.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pathRecords = run.sampledPaths.flatMap((path, idx) =>
  path.map((s, k) => ({
    t: k / run.params.nSteps,
    S: s,
    V: run.params.lambda * run.params.T * run.params.P * (1 - k / run.params.nSteps) * s,
    id: idx
  }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "normalised time  t / T", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "V_t  =  N·P·(1−t/T)·S_t    (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(pathRecords, { x: "t", y: "V", z: "id", stroke: "#888", strokeOpacity: 0.3 }),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id === 0), { x: "t", y: "V", stroke: "#c94", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Grey: sampled NAV paths. Orange: the first path, highlighted. $V_T$
pins to zero regardless of $S_T$._

### Shortfall-against-schedule on the matched treasury

The operational question on the matched treasury is not "what is
terminal P&L" (the desk total is deterministic) but *how far below
the flat-price schedule did NAV dip at the worst moment*. Formally,

$$
D := \max_{t \le T} \bigl[ N P (1 - t/T) (S_0 - S_t) \bigr]_+
$$

— paper gains do not count, only below-schedule excursions. Dashed
black marks the 95th percentile (≈ 1 year in 20), solid black the
99th (≈ 1 year in 100). Deterministic terminal P&L does not make the
matched treasury risk-free.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "max_t [N·P·(1−t/T)·(S_0−S_t)]₊   (USD shortfall)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  y: { label: "number of paths", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(run.sampleTraces.navDrawdown, Plot.binX({y:"count"}, {x: d => d, fill:"#e45", fillOpacity: 0.8})),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var95], { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var99], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Dashed black: 95th-percentile shortfall. Solid black: 99th
percentile. The natural solvency thresholds even though the matched
desk terminal P&L is deterministic._

## Parameter sweep {#parameter-sweep}

Inverting the single-run view: fix the desk composition
(partial-desk = b2b + treasury at α) and the risk statistic
(CVaR₉₅, larger = worse), and let the business parameters
$(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$ sweep. Data from `data/sweep.json`.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
sweep = FileAttachment("data/sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickAlpha = Inputs.range([0,1], { step: 0.25, value: 0.5, label: "coverage α" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMu = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.mus, { value: 0.05, label: "drift μ (/yr)" })
```

### Tail risk vs volatility $\sigma$

More volatility widens $\mathrm{Var}[I_T]$ and therefore the
partial-desk loss tail. The curve below should rise monotonically
in $\sigma$ for every $(\alpha, \mu)$; a flat or non-monotone line
is a bug.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filteredBySigma = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.alpha === pickAlpha && c.mu === pickMu)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "volatility σ   (annualised)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "linear" },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of partial desk   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, stroke: "#c53", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, fill: "#c53", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

_Partial-desk CVaR₉₅ against $\sigma$ at the selected $(\alpha, \mu)$._

### Tail risk vs coverage $\alpha$

Dose-response for pre-buying, at the middle $\sigma$ grid value.
Coverage collapses the uncovered-window integral $J_\alpha$; CVaR
drops non-linearly toward zero as $\alpha \to 1$. At $\alpha = 1$
the partial desk is deterministic, so CVaR reduces to the sign of
the matched desk's P&L — a single value, not a tail.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
byAlpha = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === sweep.grid.sigmas[1])
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Coverage α vs tail risk on the partial desk. σ fixed at the second grid value. At α = 1 the desk is deterministic so CVaR₉₅ collapses to the sign of the matched P&L.",
  x: { label: "coverage α   (0 = b2b only, 1 = fully matched)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of partial desk   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, fill: "#35a", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

## Syndicated cession and the $(1-\beta)^2$ law

The plots in this section exercise the syndicated-on-b2b operating
book. See [the syndicated book in the model](model.qmd#syndicated-book)
for the formulae. At the fair premium ($\theta = 0$),
$\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}^{\mathrm{op}} = (1 - \beta) \cdot
(\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} - \mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]) +
\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$ — an affine rescaling around the b2b
mean, slope $(1 - \beta)$. Every plot below is derived from the
sweep cells' b2b statistics; the closed-form map means **no new
Monte Carlo is needed** to price retention. The `cvar`-mode shape
factor $\phi(\Phi^{-1}(0.95))/0.05 \approx 2.063$ keeps `sharpe` and
`cvar` loads on a common SD scale.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickSigma = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.sigmas, { value: sweep.grid.sigmas[1], label: "volatility σ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickTheta = Inputs.range([0, 1], { step: 0.05, value: 0, label: "counterparty load θ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMode = Inputs.radio(["sharpe", "cvar"], { value: "sharpe", label: "premium mode" })
```

### Retained CVaR₉₅ across $\beta$

At the selected $(\mu, \sigma, \theta)$, the curve below sweeps the
cession $\beta$. The retained tail collapses linearly in $(1 -
\beta)$. Raising $\theta$ lifts the curve by the counterparty risk
premium $\beta \cdot \rho(\theta)$ — the intermediary pays mean in
exchange for the tighter tail. Composing with the treasury (not
shown here) adds another multiplicative factor tied to coverage.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
gaussianCvar95 = 2.062713055949736
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
betaGrid = Array.from({ length: 21 }, (_, i) => i / 20)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
retainedCvarCurves = {
  // Operating-layer retained: Π_ret = (1 − β)·Π_b2b + π_loaded.
  // CVaR95(Π_ret) = (1 − β)·cvar95(b2b) − β·mean(b2b) + β·θ·loadFactor·sd(b2b)
  // (the θ term lifts losses because the intermediary surrenders mean to the
  // counterparty; it adds to −E[Π_ret]).
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const rows = [];
  // Pick α = 0 so we read the retained identity on the raw b2b book.
  const cell = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell) return rows;
  const b2bMean = cell.b2b.mean;
  const b2bCvar = cell.b2b.cvar95;
  const b2bSd = cell.b2b.sd;
  for (const beta of betaGrid) {
    const fairCvar = (1 - beta) * b2bCvar - beta * b2bMean;
    const loadShift = beta * pickTheta * loadFactor * b2bSd;
    rows.push({ beta, cvar: fairCvar + loadShift });
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Retained-operating CVaR₉₅ at the selected (μ, σ, θ). β sweeps 0→1. The collapse is linear in (1 − β); θ > 0 shifts the curve upward (more tail loss) because the intermediary pays away mean.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of retained   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(retainedCvarCurves, { x: "beta", y: "cvar", stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

### Fair vs loaded premium

The fair premium $\pi_{\mathrm{fair}} = \beta \cdot
\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$ is linear in $\beta$ (the blue
$\theta = 0$ line below). A positive $\theta$ carves the loaded
premium downward by $\beta \cdot \rho(\theta)$; the vertical gap
between $\theta = 0$ and any other curve is the risk premium the
counterparty extracts for absorbing tail risk.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
premiumCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const piFair = beta * cell0.b2b.mean;
      const piLoaded = piFair - beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, pi: piLoaded, isFair: theta === 0 });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Premium paid to the intermediary as a function of β, for a handful of θ values. θ = 0 is the actuarially fair premium; larger θ carves the curve down.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "premium π   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(premiumCurves, { x: "beta", y: "pi", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

### Expected-P&L cost of syndication

The flip side of the previous plot. At $\theta = 0$, expected
retained P&L is independent of $\beta$ — the no-free-lunch
identity — and the curve below is flat at zero. For $\theta > 0$
the shortfall from the fair premium shows up as a linear drag on
$\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}] - \mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
meanShiftCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const shift = -beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, shift });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Expected-P&L cost of syndication as a function of β. At θ = 0 the curve is flat; larger θ carves linearly downward.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "E[Π_ret] − E[Π_b2b]   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(meanShiftCurves, { x: "beta", y: "shift", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

## Switching and tail truncation

The switching operating book earns the fee-book payoff whenever
$S_t \ge H = h \cdot S_0$ and the b2b payoff whenever $S_t < H$. The
book flips symmetrically as the spot crosses $H$ in either
direction — see [the switching book in the
model](model.qmd#switching-book) for the formulae.

No closed-form P&L density exists — the joint distribution of
$(T_{\mathrm{fee}}, I_{\mathrm{fee}}, I_{\mathrm{b2b}})$ is
non-trivial — so Monte Carlo is authoritative on the mean,
variance, and tails. Four mean-level anchors under pure GBM are
tracked here as z-test oracles: $\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$ (Harrison
1985), $\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$,
$\mathbb{E}[T_{\mathrm{fee}}] = \int_0^T \Phi((\nu t - \log h) /
(\sigma \sqrt{t})) \, dt$, and
$\mathbb{E}[I_{\mathrm{fee}}] = S_0 \int_0^T e^{\mu t} \Phi((\mu t +
\tfrac12 \sigma^2 t - \log h)/(\sigma \sqrt{t})) \, dt$. Merton
jumps change the τ distribution (jumps can punch through the
threshold) so under jumps the anchors drop to MC-only.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingSweep = FileAttachment("data/switching-sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingRows = switchingSweep.cells.map(c => ({
  h: c.h === null ? Infinity : c.h,
  hLabel: c.h === null ? "∞ (no switch)" : c.h.toFixed(2),
  mean: c.row.mean,
  cvar95: c.row.cvar95,
  cvar99: c.row.cvar99,
  probLoss: c.row.probLoss,
  probSwitchMc: c.switching ? c.switching.probSwitch.mc : 0,
  probSwitchCf: c.switching?.probSwitch?.closedForm ?? null,
  expectedTauMc: c.switching ? c.switching.expectedCrossingTime.mc : switchingSweep.base.T,
  expectedTauCf: c.switching?.expectedCrossingTime?.closedForm ?? null,
  expectedTauZ: c.switching?.expectedCrossingTime?.zScore ?? null,
  meanTimeInFeeMc: c.switching ? c.switching.meanTimeInFee.mc : 0,
  meanTimeInFeeCf: c.switching?.meanTimeInFee?.closedForm ?? null,
  meanTimeInFeeZ: c.switching?.meanTimeInFee?.zScore ?? null,
  meanIntegralFeeMc: c.switching ? c.switching.meanIntegralFee.mc : 0,
  meanIntegralFeeCf: c.switching?.meanIntegralFee?.closedForm ?? null,
  meanIntegralFeeZ: c.switching?.meanIntegralFee?.zScore ?? null,
  meanNCrossings: c.switching?.meanNCrossings ?? 0,
  meanFracInFeeMode: c.switching ? c.switching.meanFracInFeeMode : 0,
  retainedMean: c.retained.mean,
  retainedCvar95: c.retained.cvar95,
  feeMean: c.fee.mean,
  feeCvar95: c.fee.cvar95,
}))
```

### CVaR₉₅ and $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ as functions of the threshold

Lowering $h$ collapses CVaR₉₅ from the b2b reference (dashed
horizontal) toward the fee book's lightly negative CVaR and
simultaneously lifts $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ as losses are converted to
fee revenue. Re-entries into b2b mode (every time the spot drops
back below $H$) temper the truncation; the knee where the curve
diverges from the no-switch baseline is the operator's natural
decision point.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 60,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Switching book — CVaR₉₅ (USD loss, lower is better) as a function of threshold h. Finite h on a log scale; the dashed horizontal is the retained-on-b2b anchor. Lower h truncates more of the loss tail but also caps more of the upside.",
  x: { label: "threshold h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of switching book   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", stroke: "#a33b5b", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", fill: "#a33b5b", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? 0, t: "retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Expected P&L across thresholds. Tighter h shifts the mean upward because the fee book's positive-revenue payoff replaces b2b's mean-zero-but-fat-tailed payoff on paths that ever enter fee mode.",
  x: { label: "threshold h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "E[Π] (switching)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", stroke: "#2a7", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", fill: "#2a7", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? 0, t: "retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

### Closed-form anchors under pure GBM

For each threshold the simulator's Monte Carlo estimates of the
four mean-level anchors are z-tested against the closed forms from
`src/core/moments.ts`. The $z$ columns follow the same convention
as the main scorecard ($|z| \le 2$ healthy, $|z| > 3$ points at a
bug); the discrete-crossing bias on $\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$ and
$\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$ shrinks monotonically with $n_{\mathrm{steps}}$
(see `test/simulate-switching.test.ts`).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(
  switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)),
  {
    columns: ["hLabel", "probSwitchMc", "probSwitchCf",
              "expectedTauMc", "expectedTauCf", "expectedTauZ",
              "meanTimeInFeeMc", "meanTimeInFeeCf", "meanTimeInFeeZ",
              "meanIntegralFeeMc", "meanIntegralFeeCf", "meanIntegralFeeZ"],
    header: {
      hLabel: "h",
      probSwitchMc: "P[τ≤T] (mc)",
      probSwitchCf: "P[τ≤T] (cf)",
      expectedTauMc: "E[τ∧T] (mc)",
      expectedTauCf: "E[τ∧T] (cf)",
      expectedTauZ: "z(τ)",
      meanTimeInFeeMc: "E[T_fee] (mc)",
      meanTimeInFeeCf: "E[T_fee] (cf)",
      meanTimeInFeeZ: "z(T_fee)",
      meanIntegralFeeMc: "E[I_fee] (mc)",
      meanIntegralFeeCf: "E[I_fee] (cf)",
      meanIntegralFeeZ: "z(I_fee)",
    },
    format: {
      probSwitchMc: x => x.toFixed(3),
      probSwitchCf: x => x === null ? "—" : x.toFixed(3),
      expectedTauMc: x => x.toFixed(4),
      expectedTauCf: x => x === null ? "—" : x.toFixed(4),
      expectedTauZ: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
      meanTimeInFeeMc: x => x.toFixed(4),
      meanTimeInFeeCf: x => x === null ? "—" : x.toFixed(4),
      meanTimeInFeeZ: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
      meanIntegralFeeMc: x => x.toFixed(4),
      meanIntegralFeeCf: x => x === null ? "—" : x.toFixed(4),
      meanIntegralFeeZ: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    }
  }
)
```

### How often, and how much, the book is in fee mode

Mean fraction of the horizon spent above $H$ drops as $h$ rises and
is zero at $h = \infty$ by definition. The "E[# crossings]" column
counts sign-of-$(S - H)$ flips along the discretised path, so a
volatile path near $H$ produces many re-entries even on a single
Monte Carlo run.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(
  switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)),
  {
    columns: ["hLabel", "meanFracInFeeMode", "meanNCrossings", "mean", "cvar95"],
    header: {
      hLabel: "h",
      meanFracInFeeMode: "E[T_fee/T]",
      meanNCrossings: "E[# crossings]",
      mean: "E[Π]",
      cvar95: "CVaR95",
    },
    format: {
      meanFracInFeeMode: x => x.toFixed(3),
      meanNCrossings: x => x.toFixed(3),
      mean: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
      cvar95: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    }
  }
)
```

## The break-even quote $Q^*$ as a drift-and-horizon surface

Normalising the break-even quote by today's fee-book quote,

$$
\frac{Q^*}{(1 + f) \cdot P \cdot S_0} = \frac{e^{\mu T} - 1}{\mu T},
$$

gives a dimensionless premium (>1, b2b must charge extra to
compensate for expected appreciation) or discount (<1, expected
cheapening lets b2b quote below the fee-book price). Dashed rule at
1.0 is the $\mu = 0$ limit; longer horizons push every non-zero
curve further from that line.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstar = FileAttachment("data/qstar-surface.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstarRecords = qstar.mus.flatMap((mu, i) =>
  qstar.Ts.map((T, j) => ({ mu, T, Q: qstar.values[i][j] }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 120,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "horizon T   (years)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "Q*  /  ((1+f)·P·S₀)     (dimensionless)", labelAnchor: "center" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "drift μ (/yr)", scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([1], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      stroke: "mu",
      strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      fill: "mu",
      r: 3
    })
  ]
})
```

_One curve per drift $\mu$ on the grid. The $\mu = 0$ curve sits on
the dashed unit line; positive drift sits above, negative below._
